Based on the gathered information about agenesis specifically referring to the total absence of a structure due to the complete failure of its precursor cells, along with examples illustrating scenarios wherein certain parts might completely fail to form despite potential existence otherwise (like teeth or specific cardiac issues), we see strong alignment supporting our earlier determination. Agenesis accurately captures instances where organs or tissues entirely fail to develop properly owing solely to the unavailability of requisite initiating cells—precisely matching the description provided originally concerning organogenesis failures leading up to fully formed absences rather than partial deficiencies indicative of other conditions like aplasia or atresia.
